title: Password Reset
content:
A password is the first line of defense protecting a user's account from unauthorized access. Strong passwords are essential because they prevent malicious actors from gaining entry through guessing or automated attacks. Consumers should create passwords that combine uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters — avoiding common words, birthdates, or sequential patterns.


Examples of strong passwords:

  • Tr33-L!ne-Maple-99
  • Purple$Tiger^Window
  • 9Bridge!River@Sunset

Avoid weak patterns like Password123, qwerty, or using the same password across multiple sites.



When a Password Reset Is Needed


A consumer may request a password reset when they cannot access their account. Common reasons include:

  1. Forgotten password: The account owner has been logged out and cannot recall their credentials.
  2. Suspected compromise: The account owner believes someone else may have gained access to their account.

In all cases, the agent's priority is to protect the client's information while restoring access as quickly as possible.



Account Security Verification


Before processing any password reset, you must verify the caller's identity by asking three questions:

  1. What is your full name?
  2. What is your personal phone number on file?
  3. What are the last four digits of a payment method used on our platform?

Note: If the client has no payment method on file, skip the third question and proceed with account recovery using the first two.



Completing the Reset


Once identity is confirmed:

  1. Navigate to User Lookup and search for the client by name or email.
  2. Click their email address to open User Management.
  3. Click Send Password Reset. The system will email the client a secure reset link valid for one hour.
  4. Ask the client to check their inbox and spam folder. If the email does not arrive, you may resend it.


Before Closing the Conversation


Advise the client to strengthen their account security:

  • Enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA): This adds an extra layer of protection by requiring a one-time code from an authenticator app (such as Google Authenticator, Authy, or Microsoft Authenticator) each time they log in.
  • Use a password manager: A password manager can generate and store strong, unique passwords for every service.
  • Never share passwords: Remind the client that Tklesson staff will never ask for their password.

Confirm the client has successfully logged in before ending the conversation.
